coredata.dc:title: Mechanism of the charge separation improvement in carbon-nanodot sensitized g-C3N4
coredata.dc:creator: Ullah, Naeem
Chen, Shunwei
Zhang, Ruiqin
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), an environment-friendly metal-free photocatalysts represent a promising alternative to conventional metal-based semiconductors. Although sp2-structured carbon-dot modified g-C3N4 has shown its effectiveness to enhance the photocatalytic efficiencies, a precise, comprehensive and molecular level understanding of this enhancement is demanded for advance progress, which is lacking due to the complexity of the samples in experiments. Here, our density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT calculations reveal that a precise graphene quantum dot (GQD) sensitization of g-C3N4 nanosheet significantly enhance excited-state charge separation, which is crucial for the photocatalytic activity. Nitrogen to carbon ratio is critical for the spatial separation of photogenerated electron-hole pair via GQD/g-C3N4 π-π stacking. Red-shift in the lowest excitation of GQD/g-C3N4 with size increment stretches the absorption to the visible wavelengths, facilitating the visible-light photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. The mechanism revealed in this study supports the recent experimental reports on the competence of the environmental friendly, low cost and stable GQD sensitized g-C3N4 photocatalyst and provides guidance for optimal design to achieve higher efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4 nanosheet
Graphene quantum dot
Photocatalyst
Charge-separation
TDDFT

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic view of the (a) C16H10 (pyrene), C24H12 (coronene) (b) heptazine (CN), 6-CN and (c) the front and side view of the C24H12 over 6-CN (C24H12/6-CN). The representative colored balls indicate carbon, nitrogen and hydrogen atoms.
Spatial distributions of the HOMO-LUMO orbitals of 6-CN, C16H10/6-CN and C24H12/6-CN at the S0 and S1 geometries.

Relaxed ground- and excited-state electronic structure of 6-CN and GQDs/6-CN nanocomposite. (a) Schematic view of the calculated transition orbitals pairs contributing at the ground- and excited-state geometries, (b) corresponding electron-hole distributions at the relaxed excited-state geometry. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
HOMO and LUMO distributions of (a) GQD/3-CN, (b) GQD/6-CN (c) GQD/10-CN and (d) GQD/15-CN at S0 and S1 geometries.
Electron-hole (orange-red) distribution at S1 geometries of GQD/3-CN, GQD/6-CN, GQD/10-CN, and GQD/21-CN.

Nitrogen to carbon (N:C) ratio as a function of the molecular mass ratio of GQD to g-C3N4 (MGQD/Mg-C3N4) in GQD/g-C3N4 system. Control over the N:C ratio regulated the electron-hole separation in the modified g-C3N4 structure.

The orbitals contributions to the lowest excitation at S0 and S1 relaxed geometries. The corresponding excitation energies(wavelengths). The ground state adsorption energies of GQD on g-C3N4 nanosheet for each structure are also given.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Contribution of orbitals above 15% is considered in the above table.

head.ce:title: Mechanism of the charge separation improvement in carbon-nanodot sensitized g-C3N4

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Research Institute, City University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen 518057, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Computational Science Research Center, Beijing 100193, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), an environment-friendly metal-free photocatalysts represent a promising alternative to conventional metal-based semiconductors. Although sp2-structured carbon-dot modified g-C3N4 has shown its effectiveness to enhance the photocatalytic efficiencies, a precise, comprehensive and molecular level understanding of this enhancement is demanded for advance progress, which is lacking due to the complexity of the samples in experiments. Here, our density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT calculations reveal that a precise graphene quantum dot (GQD) sensitization of g-C3N4 nanosheet significantly enhance excited-state charge separation, which is crucial for the photocatalytic activity. Nitrogen to carbon ratio is critical for the spatial separation of photogenerated electron-hole pair via GQD/g-C3N4 π-π stacking. Red-shift in the lowest excitation of GQD/g-C3N4 with size increment stretches the absorption to the visible wavelengths, facilitating the visible-light photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. The mechanism revealed in this study supports the recent experimental reports on the competence of the environmental friendly, low cost and stable GQD sensitized g-C3N4 photocatalyst and provides guidance for optimal design to achieve higher efficiency.

GQD sensitized g-C3N4 nanosheet showed enhanced photogenerated electron-hole separation. Appropriate nitrogen to carbon (N:C) ratio regulated photogenerated electron-hole separation inspires the experimentalists to synthesis an appropriate GQD sensitized g-C3N4 for the enhanced photocatalytic performance.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-energy driven photocatalytic reactions act as a favorable path to overcome the increasing energy and environmental concerns [1–4]. The use of the solar-energy for photocatalytic reactions strongly depends on the sunlight absorption capability of the photocatalyst and the conductivity for the photogenerated charge carries. So far, metal oxides, sulfides and oxynitrides with optical-range sensitivity have been successfully fabricated through band structure engineering [5–8]. Among them, TiO2 emerged as an excellent photocatalyst [9] and long been studied for ultraviolet light activity [10,11]. However, its wide band gap nature restricts its light response to a very narrow region, which greatly reduces its solar energy utilization [12]. To overcome the limitation, visible range-driven photocatalysts have witnessed the attention of the research community in the last decade [13]. As a sustainable material, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been aimed by the research community amid its graphene-like two-dimensional (2D) structure, suitable band gap (2.7 eV), visible light absorption, chemical, and thermal stabilities, nonmetal nature and photocatalytic properties [14]. Though, great progress in the g-C3N4 synthesis has been made, the low photo-conversion efficiency in the visible-light range and high recombination rate of the photo-generated electron-hole pair imposes severe limitations on the photocatalytic performance. A promising method to improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 through surface junction for charge separation is needed. This can be accomplished through decorating the g-C3N4 with metal or carbon nanomaterials for effective capturing of the photogenerated electrons for enhanced catalytic performance [15–18]. On the other hand, the semiconductor heterostructure band alignment approach showed promising results [19–23]. However, the toxic metals leaching in these composite systems emerge as a major obstacle. Hence, metal-free and sustainable materials-based g-C3N4 photocatalytic systems are needed for photocatalytic applications.

Carbon dots with predominant graphitic carbon offer promising visible optical property due to their tunable band gap [24] and have recently attracted great attention because of their optoelectronic [25,26], photoluminescent [27] and photogenerated charge carrier properties [28]. Experimentally, carbon dot has been successfully loaded to effectively sensitize TiO2 nanorods for improved photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance under visible light irradiation [29]. A hybrid graphene/gC3N4 nanocomposite has been successfully synthesized [30] showing an enhanced photocatalytic activity upon visible light irradiation. Computations of the bulk hybrid graphene and graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposite showed enhanced optical absorption featuring its novel photovoltaic and photocatalytic applications [31]. Considering the pi-conjugated structure of g-C3N4 and GQDs, the combination of these two has been reported by several studies [12,32–34]. Fang et al. [32] demonstrated that graphene quantum dot (GQD) modified g-C3N4 possesses enhanced photoreactivity compared to the pristine g-C3N4. Liu et al. [12] reported the design and fabrication of the metal-free GQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposite with impressive performance for the photocatalytic water splitting. In another experimental report, sp2-structured carbon dot decorated g-C3N4 revealed enhanced reactivity in photocatalytic phenol degradation and charge separation [33]. Still, the molecular level study and mechanism of the excited-state orbitals and electron-hole character need comprehensive investigation for the advancement of cost-effective carbon-dot sensitized g-C3N4 system. In our previous experimental effort [35], we have shown that the increase of sp2 carbon content due to nitrogen evaporation in g-C3N4 through annealing led to the electron-delocalization enhanced photocurrent density. In another work, we have increased sp2 carbon content in a g-CN film by polymerizing melamine with 2, 6-diaminopyridine, achieving considerably improved PEC performance of the carbon nitride films with photon-to-current efficiency of 16% at 400 nm for enhanced PEC applications [36]. In this work, we have designed GQD sensitized g-C3N4 sheet and explored the optical properties and electron-hole separation through density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations. Our results reveal that the physisorption of GQDs on the g-C3N4 surface resulting in the enhanced photoexcited electron-hole separation and broadened visible light absorption. Importantly, the charge localization and electron-hole separation have been corroborated through the study of the excited-state (S1) orbitals and electron-hole computations. The revealed molecular structural information highlights the mechanism and cause for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the GQDs sensitized g-C3N4 as revealed in experiments and provides the interpretation concerning the further experimental advancements.

The ground- and excited-state properties of the GQD/g-C3N4 investigated by using the DFT and TDDFT methods, as implemented in the Gaussian 09 package [37]. The range-separated ωB97XD hybrid functional [38] with the 6-31G(d,p) basis set [39] was selected for all the calculations. The accuracy of ωB97XD hybrid functional has recently been validated for the study of π conjugated systems [40,41]. Models of GQD modified g-C3N4 nanosheets are shown in Fig. S1 (in Electronic supplementary information file, ESI: 3-CN, 5-CN, 6-CN, 7-CN, 10-CN, 15-CN and 21-CN), where numerical value denotes the number of heptazine units and CN denotes the nanosheet. To check the stability of the adsorbed GQD on g-C3N4 nanosheets, the adsorption energy E
Ads for each structure is calculated according to the equation,
E Ads
=
E
GQD / g −
C 3
N 4
−
E GQD
+
E
g −
C 3
N 4
where E
GQD/g−C 3 N 4 and E
g−C 3 N 4 are the total energies of the GQD sensitized g-C3N4 and individual g-C3N4 nanosheet, E
GQD is the total energy of an isolated GQD. The adsorption energy values, −0.87, −1.13, −1.06, −0.93, −1.16, −1.22 and − 1.07 in electronvolt (eV) are calculated for the 3-CN, 5-CN, 6-CN, 7-CN, 10-CN, 15-CN and 21-CN respectively. These values show stability of the modified systems as revealed by the previous experimental [12,32–34] and theoretical [42] studies. The stable configurations indicate strong interaction between the GQD and g-C3N4 nanosheet, in the GQD sensitized g-C3N4 nanosheet.

The lowest absorption energy (Eabs) was determined as the first vertical excitation energy calculated by the TDDFT method based on the optimized ground-state (S0) geometry; the emission energy (Eemi), following the Kasha's rule [43], was determined as the S1 → S0 transition energy by optimizing the S1 geometry. The electron-hole distribution is analyzed by using the Multiwfn program [44,45]. The excited state can be expressed from the configuration interaction state given by;
Ψ exc
=
∑
i → l
w i l
Φ i l
where i and l run over all the occupied and unoccupied virtual molecular orbitals respectively. Φ i
l represents the configuration state wavefunction and w
i
l is the configuration coefficient. The electron and hole density distribution are then calculated through the following relations;
ρ e
=
∑
i → l
w i l
2
φ l
φ l
+
∑
i → l
∑
i → m ≠ l
w i l
w i m
φ l
φ m
ρ h
=
∑
i → l
w i l
2
φ i
φ i
+
∑
i → l
∑
j ≠ i → l
w i l
w j l
φ i
φ j
where ρ e and ρ h are electron and hole density distributions. i, j and l, m represent the occupied and virtual molecular orbitals (denoted by φ) respectively [45]. The first term stands for the local contribution while the second term is the cross contribution for transition from several states. The excited-state geometries of the larger GQD sensitized g-C3N4 nanosheets (GQD/10-CN, GQD/15-CN, GQD/21-CN) were fully optimized by the self-consistent-charge density-functional tight-binding dispersion (SCC-DFTB-D) method [45,46]. The DFTB-D method is well known for having high computational efficiency to deal with large systems containing hundreds of atoms, and it is particularly suitable for systems containing C, H, O, and N atoms [46,47].

Recent experimental studies proposed the π-π stacking of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with layered g-C3N4 sheets, preventing the formation of defects and provide a superior contact interface [12,32–34]. As shown in Fig. 1 , we selected sp2 structured carbon dots, pyrene (C16H10) and coronene (C24H12) for the physisorption on the g-C3N4 nanosheet composed of the six heptazine (building-block of the g-C3N4) units (6-CN). The schematic front- and side-view of the C24H12 over 6-CN (C24H12/6-CN) is shown in Fig. 1(c). To elaborate the effect of GQDs on the electronic structure of g-C3N4 nanosheet, we calculated the ground- (S0) and excited-state (S1) frontier orbitals (FOs) using TDDFT method, which are shown in Fig. 2 . For comparison, we provided the 6-CN nanosheet (NS) and its GQDs modulated C16H10/6-CN and C24H12/6-CN structures. From figure, it can be seen that the HOMO predominantly distributes on GQDs while LUMO distributes on the carbon nitride sheet, facilitating the charge separation upon excitation which is crucial for the photocatalysis [21]. On the other hand, the ground-state frontier orbitals in 6-CN are fused, while there is eminent separation after excited-state relaxation. Significant spatial orbital separation in GQD sensitized g-C3N4 nanosheet will facilitate the electron-hole separation as revealed in experiments [12,33].

Before interpreting electron-hole distributions, we provide a schematic view of the calculated transitions of orbitals pairs involving the ground-state and at the relaxed excited-state geometries in 6-CN, C16H10/6-CN and C24H12/6-CN systems as shown in Fig. 3(a). Black (red) colored bands represent the ground- and excited-state orbitals of the GQD sensitized g-C3N4. The first excitation is labeled via the black-arrows from the occupied orbitals to the unoccupied orbitals while the corresponding contributions from pair of orbitals are also given. Only pair of orbitals with more than 15% of contribution to the transition is designated in the diagram. Spatial distributions of the orbitals involved in the respective S0–S1 and S1–S0 transitions are shown in Figs. S2, S3, S4 (Electronic supplementary information (ESI) file) respectively for the 6-CN, C16H10/6-CN, C24H12/6-CN systems. The excitation from S0 to S1 is accompanied by the structural relaxation of the structures in the S1 state. Interestingly, structural relaxation in S1 prominently influenced the contribution to the allowed transitions from the various orbitals of the modified structures. Frontier orbitals are the major contributors in the excited-state transitions, where HOMO majorly covered by the carbon moiety while the virtual orbitals are predominantly from 6-CN nanosheet (Figs. S2, S3, S4). From Fig. 3(a), we observe that the GQDs significantly influence the electronic states and occupy the highest occupied state in the sensitized structure and hence play an important role in the excitations, and charge separation. Electronic structures of this type substantially facilitate the excited-state charge separation and mobility inhibition making them prospect for the photocatalytic process. Taking advantage of the suitable electronic structures for excited-state charge separation, we thus mainly focus on the electron and hole distributions at the relaxed S1 geometry.

For excited-state charge separation, we have performed computational studies of the electron and hole involved in the first excitation for the GQDs/g-C3N4 assembly. The excited-state charge separation is interpreted from the distributions of the electron and hole at the relaxed geometry of S1 state [48,49]. In Fig. 3(b), we have shown the electron-hole involved in the first excitation for 6-CN, C16H10/6-CN and C24H12/6-CN structures on the S1 energy surface. As shown, hole distributed over the sp2 carbon moiety ensuring the active character of the GQDs as a sensitizer of the g-C3N4 nanosheet while absorbing the light. After excitation, electron is mainly distributed on the 6-CN sheet causing the enhanced charge separation. The electron and hole distributions are in line with the structure of orbitals involved in the relaxed S1 state transitions. In the sensitized structure, an electron can now be directly excited from occupied orbitals of GQD(s) to the virtual orbitals of 6-CN sheet. Previously, reports on the graphene/titania complex have clearly demonstrated that such type of system mechanizes the enhanced optical activity [50]. On the device electrode scale, the enhanced charge separation will facilitate the enriched photocurrent due to the transport of the photogenerated electrons and holes detention by the hole rescuers in the electrolyte. Also, the electron-hole distributions in 6-CN structure are diffused, inhibiting the transport of the photogenerated electrons and hole, instead of recombining them. This type of fast recombination supports the experimental findings regarding the low photocatalytic efficiency of the g-C3N4. These observations are in line with the recent experimental demonstration that the GQDs facilitate electron-hole separation and inhibit the recombination in g-C3N4 to accumulate the photocatalytic reaction [32–34]. This is steady confirmation for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the GQDs modified g-C3N4. It is thus of great importance that further reports ascertain the over-all electron-hole separation in GQDs modified g-C3N4 on the experimental side to achieve high photocatalytic activity.

GQDs incorporation to the g-C3N4 sheet manifests enhancement in the spatial charge separation and eventually the photocatalytic performance, still, the concentration of the incorporated-GQDs is an important aspect that will alter the physical properties of g-C3N4. Recent experimental investigations [12,32] reported modification in the photocatalytic efficiency of the system upon changing the GQD concentration in over the g-C3N4 nanosheet. To have a clearer interpretation of the concentration dependence of the charge separation and hence the photocatalytic capability, we have modelled several structures while modifying the size of g-C3N4 sheet in the GQD/g-C3N4 system to alter the molecular mass ratio of GQD to g-C3N4 sheet (MGQD/Mg-C3N4). We started with GQD/3-CN sensitized structure having g-C3N4 part with three heptazines (3-CN) and coronene (as GQD), where MGQD/Mg-C3N4 is 4.8 × 10−1 and enlarged the g-C3N4 sheets by adding more heptazines (detailed CN structures are given in Fig. S1. The influence on the electronic structure is studied through the calculation of the frontier orbital (FO) at the ground (S0) and first excited-state (S1) relaxed geometries as shown in Fig. 4(a). From figure, the HOMO is mainly on the GQD while the LUMO is distributed over both parts of the assembly. Both ground- and excited-state showed that the GQD influenced the HOMO-LUMO distributions and the delocalized FO structure with the dominant character of the GQD sensitizer. We next study the GQD/6-CN (already discussed above as C24H12/6-CN) with MGQD/Mg-C3N4 ratio of 2.9 × 10−1 which shows localized FOs display where the HOMO (LUMO) are solely on the GQD (6-CN) parts of the π-π conjugated structure in both S0 and S1 states (Fig. 4(b)). The FO localization and sole occupation of the HOMO and LUMO by the respective parts show electronic structures significantly influenced by modifying the MGQD/Mg-C3N4 ratio.

Demonstration of the size-influence with models ranging from 3-CN to 6-CN on the electronic structures requires calculations of FOs of enlarged g-C3N4 sheets with models of 10-heptazines (MGQD/Mg-C3N4 = 1.5 × 10−1), 15-heptazines (MGQD/Mg-C3N4 = 1.0 × 10−1) and 21-heptazines (MGQD/Mg-C3N4 = 7.2 × 10−2). It is expected that size increment in the g-C3N4 part will essentially impact and regulate the electronic structures and the HOMO and LUMO occupation. The calculated HOMO and LUMO orbitals in the ground- and excited-state are shown in Fig. 4(c, d). Again, the HOMO-LUMO orbitals mainly locate on the GQD and g-C3N4 sheet respectively, revealing the dominant character of the π-π conjugation. For the electron-hole separation, the orbital contribution to the vertical excitation is of central importance, as it eventually reveals the contribution from different parts of the GQD sensitized g-C3N4 subjected to the variation in the number of the heptazines. Table 1 summarizes the contribution of the pair of orbitals to first vertical excitation obtained at the relaxed S0 and S1 geometries for all considered systems in this study. It should be noted that the GQD/5-CN and GQD/7-CN systems are introduced here to see the influence of the g-C3N4 part without having the initial geometry with triangular shape and will be discussed in the following paragraphs. Size-dependence of the first vertical excitation energies is demonstrated, where size increment of the number of heptazines declined the vertical excitation energies; a usual character shown by the carbon and carbon-nitride nanostructures [51,52]. As revealed by experiments [12,32], the absorption wavelength span over 375–464 nm region, enlarging the coverage of the visible region wavelengths. We have carried out optimization on the S1 energy surface using TDDFT method to examine the influence on the orbital contribution to the coupling between S0 and S1 states and the emission energies. From the table, it is evident that the S1 geometry relaxation influences S1–S0 transition and hence the FOs distributions; we expect the electron-hole separation will be influenced by the relative increase in the number of the heptazines.

A prominent property of charge-separation essentially influenced by the GQD concentration on the g-C3N4 surface; reported by numerous experimental studies [12,32–34]. In Fig. 5 , we have shown the electron-hole distribution in GQD/3-CN, GQD/6-CN, GQD/10-CN and GQD/21-CN on the S1 energy surface to regularize the charge separation with size variations of g-C3N4 sheet via increasing the number of heptazines. In GQD/3-CN, the electron-hole mainly distributes on the carbon-dot moiety, eventually acting as the recombination centers. This is consistent with the recent reports by Fang et al. [32] and Zhang et al. [33] where they demonstrated that too much GQD will act as recombination centers and will reduce the photoreactivity of g-C3N4 sheet. With an increase in the g-C3N4 sheet to 6-heptazines, the system showed very good charge separation, where hole occupies GQD and electrons are concentrated on the 6-CN sheet. Upon further increase of the g-C3N4 nanosheet to 10-heptazines, the electrons and holes are still separated with small diffusion as seen on the g-C3N4 part; this can be attributed to the increase in g-C3N4 part. On expansion to the 21-heptazines, g-C3N4 content dominates and hence the electron-hole primarily distributed over the g-C3N4 part showing the diffused excited-state charges acting as the fast electron-hole recombination centers. Electron-hole distributions in the GQD/5-CN, GQD/7-CN and GQD/15-CN structures are also illustrated in Fig. S5. Hence size-regulation of the electron-hole separation in the GQD sensitized g-C3N4 demonstrated an important role in determining the photocatalytic performance.

An inspection of the influence of shape of g-C3N4 part and the nitrogen to carbon (N:C) ratio on the electron and hole distribution was further carried out for the GQD sensitized systems. We have designed the GQD/5-CN with 5-heptazines and GQD/7-CN with 7-heptazines models, with the N:C ratio close to that of the GQD/6-CN (with very good electron-hole separation) as shown in the Fig. S5. Both structures showed excellent electron-hole separation, consistent with that result of the GQD/6-CN. Based on the size-regulated charge separation, we plotted nitrogen to carbon ratio as function of the GQD to g-C3N4 molecular-mass ratio, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . A large N:C ratio corresponds to the main contribution from the g-C3N4 nanosheet of GQD/21-CN system with a lower MGQD/Mg-C3N4 ratio, exhibiting the dominant character of g-C3N4. Similarly, the lowest N:C ratio of the GQD/3-CN shown dominant character of GQDs acting as the fast recombination center instead of the electron-hole separated distributions. Remarkably, the N:C ratio of 0.8–1.0 with corresponding molecular mass ratio 1.5 × 10−1–3.0 × 10−1 yields in the enhanced electron-hole separation, complying to the recent experimental reports [12,34] on the nitrogen to carbon ration regulated charge separation in sp2 bonded carbon dots sensitized g-C3N4. The revealed molecular level information on the enhanced electron-hole separation is valuable to guide the systematic synthesis of an appropriate and low-cost carbon nanodot sensitized g-C3N4 for the enhanced photocatalytic performance.

DFT and TDDFT investigations have been performed to evaluate the influence of GQD on the sensitized g-C3N4 nanosheets. Examination of the electronic structures and photoexcited charge separation can be revealed by the excited-state electron-hole distribution in the GQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposite system. The GQD incorporation to g-C3N4 sheet significantly influences the electronic structures and induces enhanced excited-state charge separation, showing a suitable podium for the photocatalytic activity. The variation of the concentration of number of heptazine through nitrogen to carbon (N:C) ratio regulates the electron-hole separation, guiding the design of appropriate GQD assembly over g-C3N4 nanosheets for enhanced photocatalysis. GQD sensitization of the g-C3N4 nanosheet inhibits the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, facilitating the photocatalytic activity of the g-C3N4. This study regulates the photoexcited electron-hole pair separation through the amount of the GQD required for the feasible sensitization of g-C3N4, which will inspire the experimentalist to prepare more efficient metal-free photocatalysts and stimulate their practical applications in photocatalysis.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this paper was supported by grants from the Research Grants Council, University Grants Committee of the Hong Kong SAR [Project No. CityU 11305618], the Guangdong-Hong Kong Technology Cooperation Funding Scheme (2017A050506048) and the Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality (JCYJ20160229165210666 and JCYJ20170818104105891).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This file contains the Heptazine (CN) based g-C3N4 representative structures (Fig. S1), Spatial distribution of the orbitals involved in the respective S0−S1 and S1−S0 transitions for g-C3N4 nanosheet and GQD sensitzed g-C3N4 nanosheets (Figs. S2, S3, S4), the electron-hole distributions in excited-state of GQD/5-CN, GQD/7-CN and GQD/15-CN (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.05.032.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct splitting of water under visible light irradiation with an oxide semiconductor photocatalyst
Heterogeneous photocatalyst materials for water splitting
